walkthrough.title: Unfold the Game Board
walkthrough.text: Let's start by unfolding the large game board and placing it in the center of the table. The board shows a grid of action spaces organized into columns. Some spaces are already printed on the board and available from the start, while the top row has empty slots where round cards will be placed.

walkthrough.title: Organize the Round Cards
walkthrough.text: Let's separate the round cards into six stages. Stage one has four cards, stage two has three, stage three has two, stage four has two, stage five has two, and stage six has one card for a total of fourteen rounds.

walkthrough.title: Shuffle and Place Round Cards
walkthrough.text: Let's shuffle the cards within each stage separately, then stack them in order with stage one on top. Place this face-down deck near the round card slots on the board. We will reveal one card per round throughout the game.

walkthrough.title: Sort the Resource Supply
walkthrough.text: Let's organize all the resource tokens into separate supply piles near the board. You need piles for wood, clay, reed, stone, grain, and vegetables. Keep them accessible to all players.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Animal Supply
walkthrough.text: Let's sort the animal tokens into three separate piles by type. You will need sheep, wild boar, and cattle. Place these supply piles near the board where everyone can reach them.

walkthrough.title: Set Out Food and Begging Tokens
walkthrough.text: Let's place the food tokens in a central supply pile. Also set out the begging cards nearby. You never want to take begging cards, but they need to be available as a penalty for players who cannot feed their families at harvest.

walkthrough.title: Distribute Farmyard Boards
walkthrough.text: Let's give each player a personal farmyard board. This board shows a three-by-five grid of fifteen spaces. Your farm starts mostly empty and you will fill it with rooms, fields, pastures, and stables over the course of the game.

walkthrough.title: Place Starting Rooms and Workers
walkthrough.text: Let's place two wooden room tiles on the left side of each farmyard board. Then place one person token in each room, giving every player two family members to start. These are your workers for the entire game.

walkthrough.title: Distribute Fences and Stables
walkthrough.text: Let's give each player fifteen wooden fence pieces and four stable tokens in their player color. These will be used to build pastures and stables on your farmyard board to house animals.

walkthrough.title: Lay Out Major Improvement Cards
walkthrough.text: Let's place the ten major improvement cards face up near the board. These include the Fireplace, Cooking Hearth, Well, Clay Oven, Stone Oven, and Joinery among others. Any player can build these during the game, but each card can only be claimed once.

walkthrough.title: Deal Occupation and Minor Improvement Cards
walkthrough.text: Let's shuffle the occupation deck and deal seven cards to each player. Then shuffle the minor improvement deck and deal seven cards to each player as well. These fourteen cards form your private hand for the entire game.

walkthrough.title: Stock the Accumulation Spaces
walkthrough.text: Let's place the starting resources on the board's accumulation spaces. Some action spaces gather resources each round. For example, the three wood space starts with three wood tokens on it. Check each accumulation space and place its initial resources.

walkthrough.title: Give Starting Food
walkthrough.text: Let's distribute starting food to each player. The start player receives two food, and each other player receives three food. This slight advantage for later players helps balance the power of going first.

walkthrough.title: Determine Start Player
walkthrough.text: Let's choose a start player and give them the start player token. That player will place the first worker each round until someone takes the Start Player action space. Now we are ready to begin round one.

summary.title: Unfold the Game Board
bullets: Action spaces visible to all players

summary.title: Shuffle and Place Round Cards
bullets: Stack in order, stage 1 on top

summary.title: Sort the Resource Supply
bullets: Separate piles: wood, clay, reed, stone, grain, vegetables

summary.title: Set Out Food and Begging Tokens
Place Starting Rooms and Workers
bullets: 2 wooden rooms on left side of farmyard
1 family member per room → 2 workers total

summary.title: Lay Out Major Improvement Cards
bullets: Shared pool — first come, first served

summary.title: Deal Occupation and Minor Improvement Cards
Stock the Accumulation Spaces
bullets: Place initial resources on accumulation action spaces

summary.title: Determine Start Player
bullets: Start player places first worker each round

walkthrough.title: Game Overview
walkthrough.text: Let's understand the big picture. Agricola is a farming game played over fourteen rounds grouped into six stages. Your goal is to build the most well-rounded farm by plowing fields, raising animals, growing your family, and improving your home.

walkthrough.title: Round Structure
walkthrough.text: Let's walk through what happens each round. First, we reveal the top round card and place it on the board, opening a new action space. Then we replenish all accumulation spaces with their listed resources.

walkthrough.title: Work Phase
walkthrough.text: Let's talk about the core of the game. Starting with the start player and going clockwise, each player places one family member on an unoccupied action space and immediately takes that action. We keep going around until everyone has placed all their workers.

walkthrough.title: One Worker Per Space
walkthrough.text: Let's remember the most fundamental rule. Each action space can hold only one worker per round, no matter which player placed it. This means if someone takes the space you wanted, you must choose a different action. Planning and timing are everything.

walkthrough.title: Accumulation Spaces
walkthrough.text: Let's look at how accumulation works. Some action spaces add resources every round whether or not anyone takes them. If nobody takes the three wood space for two rounds, it will have six wood waiting. The longer you wait, the bigger the payout.

walkthrough.title: Gathering Resources
walkthrough.text: Let's cover the basic resource actions. You can send a worker to collect wood, clay, reed, stone, or food from various spaces on the board. These resources are the building blocks for everything you will construct on your farm.

walkthrough.title: Plowing Fields
walkthrough.text: Let's learn about plowing. The Plow Field action lets you place a field tile on any empty space of your farmyard. Fields are where you will sow grain and vegetables. You must plow a field before you can plant anything on it.

walkthrough.title: Sowing Crops
walkthrough.text: Let's understand sowing. When you take the Sow action, you place grain or vegetable tokens from your supply onto your plowed fields. Grain is planted as three tokens stacked on a field, and vegetables as two tokens. During each harvest, you remove one token from each sown field.

walkthrough.title: Building Rooms
walkthrough.text: Let's talk about expanding your home. You can build additional rooms onto your house by paying resources matching your current room type. Wooden rooms cost five wood and two reed, clay rooms cost five clay and two reed, and stone rooms cost five stone and two reed. New rooms must be adjacent to existing ones.

walkthrough.title: Growing Your Family
walkthrough.text: Let's discuss family growth. You can only add a new family member if you have an empty room. The new person is placed in the empty room and becomes an additional worker starting next round. More workers means more actions per round, which is extremely powerful.

walkthrough.title: Building Fences and Pastures
walkthrough.text: Let's learn about animal housing. You can build fences around empty farmyard spaces to create pastures. A pasture is any fully enclosed area and can hold two animals of one type. You may build multiple pastures in a single fencing action.

walkthrough.title: Stables and Animal Capacity
walkthrough.text: Let's understand stables. A stable placed inside a pasture doubles that pasture's animal capacity. An unfenced stable on its own holds exactly one animal. You can also keep one pet animal in your home without any pasture, as long as it is just one.

walkthrough.title: Taking Animals
walkthrough.text: Let's talk about getting animals. When you take an animal action, you receive animals from the supply. You must immediately have legal housing for every animal you take. If you cannot house them, they are returned to the supply and lost.

walkthrough.title: Renovation
walkthrough.text: Let's cover home upgrades. The Renovate action lets you upgrade all your rooms at once from wood to clay, or from clay to stone. You pay one of the new material per room plus one reed total. Higher room types score more points at game end.

walkthrough.title: Occupations and Minor Improvements
walkthrough.text: Let's look at your hand cards. Occupations give you ongoing abilities or bonuses and cost food to play. Minor improvements provide permanent upgrades to your farm and cost various resources. Both card types are played using specific action spaces on the board.

walkthrough.title: Major Improvements
walkthrough.text: Let's discuss the shared improvement cards. Major improvements like the Fireplace and Cooking Hearth let you convert animals and resources into food at any time. The Clay Oven and Stone Oven give food when you sow. Only one player can own each major improvement.

walkthrough.title: Cooking and Food Conversion
walkthrough.text: Let's understand cooking. Once you have a cooking improvement like a Fireplace or Cooking Hearth, you can convert animals and resources into food at any time, not just during your turn. This is crucial for feeding your family at harvest time.

walkthrough.title: Harvest Overview
walkthrough.text: Let's walk through harvest. Harvest happens after rounds four, seven, nine, eleven, thirteen, and fourteen. It has three phases that always occur in order: field phase, feeding phase, then breeding phase.

walkthrough.title: Harvest Field Phase
walkthrough.text: Let's start with the field phase. You remove one grain or one vegetable token from each of your sown fields and add it to your personal supply. These harvested crops can be used as food or saved for future sowing.

walkthrough.title: Harvest Feeding Phase
walkthrough.text: Let's cover the critical feeding phase. Each family member requires two food. You must pay a total of two food per person from your supply. If you cannot pay the full amount, you take one begging card for each missing food, and each begging card is worth negative three points.

walkthrough.title: Harvest Breeding Phase
walkthrough.text: Let's finish with breeding. If you have at least two animals of the same type, you receive one baby animal of that type from the supply. You must have room to house the baby. Breeding happens for each animal type separately: sheep, boar, and cattle.

walkthrough.title: End of Game Scoring
walkthrough.text: Let's review how scoring works after round fourteen. You score points across many categories in scoring bands. Fields score zero to four points, pastures zero to four, grain zero to eight, vegetables zero to four. Each animal type scores in its own band as well.

walkthrough.title: Scoring Penalties and Bonuses
walkthrough.text: Let's cover the other scoring elements. You lose one point for every unused farmyard space. You gain one point per fenced stable, one point per clay room, two points per stone room, and three points per family member. Card bonuses and begging card penalties are also added.

walkthrough.title: Winning the Game
walkthrough.text: Let's wrap up the rules. The player with the highest total score wins. The key to Agricola is balance. You are penalized for having nothing in any category, so a well-rounded farm almost always beats one that specializes too heavily in just one area.

summary.title: Game Overview
bullets: Build the most well-rounded farm to win

walkthrough.title: Welcome to the Practice Farm
walkthrough.text: Let's play through the opening rounds of a four-player game together. We will follow four farmers named Anna, Ben, Clara, and David as they build their farms. Along the way, we will see some common mistakes and learn how to avoid them.

walkthrough.title: Starting Position
walkthrough.text: Let's look at what everyone begins with. Each player has a farmyard with two wooden rooms and two family members. Anna is the start player with two food, while Ben, Clara, and David each have three food.

walkthrough.title: Round One Begins
walkthrough.text: Let's reveal the first round card and add it to the board. Now we replenish the accumulation spaces, placing three wood on the three wood space, one clay on the clay space, one reed on the reed space, and one food on the fishing space. Anna goes first.

walkthrough.title: Anna Takes Wood
walkthrough.text: Let's watch Anna place her first worker on the three wood accumulation space. She collects all three wood tokens. This is a smart early move because wood is needed for rooms, fences, and stables. Ben is next.

walkthrough.title: Ben Takes Clay
walkthrough.text: Let's see Ben place a worker on the clay space and collect one clay. Clay is useful for renovating rooms and building improvements like the Fireplace. Clara is up next.

walkthrough.title: Clara Plows a Field
walkthrough.text: Let's watch Clara send a worker to the Plow Field action. She places a field tile on her farmyard. This is great long-term planning because she can sow grain or vegetables on it later to create a food engine.

walkthrough.title: David Takes Day Laborer
walkthrough.text: Let's see David place a worker on the Day Laborer space, taking two food. With three starting food plus two more, David now has five food. That is enough to feed his two workers at the first harvest, but he is not building toward anything yet.

walkthrough.title: Second Workers Go Out
walkthrough.text: Let's continue the round as each player places their second worker. Anna takes reed, Ben takes the grain seed action and collects one grain, Clara takes the Day Laborer for food, and David goes fishing to collect the accumulated food token.

walkthrough.title: Round Two Actions
walkthrough.text: Let's move into round two. A new round card is revealed, accumulation spaces refill, and work begins again. Anna grabs the three wood space which now has three fresh wood. Ben takes clay again. Clara sows her plowed field with grain, placing three grain tokens on it.

walkthrough.title: David's Mistake: Premature Family Growth
walkthrough.text: Let's watch David make a common beginner error. He sees a Family Growth action space has appeared on a round card and immediately takes it, building a new room and adding a third family member. He now has three workers but only three food left, and no cooking improvement or food engine.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Build Your Food Engine First
walkthrough.text: Let's understand why David's move was a problem. With three family members, he needs six food at harvest. He has no fields sown, no cooking improvement, and only three food saved. Growing your family is powerful, but you need a reliable food source in place first, or you will be scrambling to avoid begging cards.

walkthrough.title: Round Three Progress
walkthrough.text: Let's jump to round three. Anna has been collecting wood and now has enough to build fences. Ben picks up the Fireplace major improvement using his clay, giving him the ability to cook animals into food. Clara harvests one grain from her sown field during a Bake Bread action.

walkthrough.title: Clara Builds Fences
walkthrough.text: Let's watch Clara take the Build Fences action. She uses six wood to build fences around two farmyard spaces, creating a pasture that can hold two sheep. She is preparing for the sheep that will become available in later rounds.

walkthrough.title: Ben's Mistake: Animals Without Housing
walkthrough.text: Let's see Ben make a painful error. A Sheep action space appeared this round offering one sheep. Ben eagerly takes it, forgetting he has no pastures, no stables, and already has his one allowed house pet. The sheep has nowhere to go and must be returned to the supply.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Prepare Housing Before Taking Animals
walkthrough.text: Let's learn from Ben's mistake. Animals need legal housing the moment you take them. A pasture holds two, a stable in a pasture doubles that, and an unfenced stable holds one. Always build your fences or stables before you send a worker to collect animals, or they go right back to the supply.

walkthrough.title: Round Four and First Harvest
walkthrough.text: Let's reach the end of stage one. Round four is complete, and now harvest happens for the first time. Everyone pauses to handle the three harvest phases: field phase, feeding phase, and breeding phase.

walkthrough.title: Field Phase
walkthrough.text: Let's harvest the fields. Clara removes one grain from her sown field, adding it to her personal supply. She still has one grain left on the field for next harvest. No other player has sown fields, so they skip this phase.

walkthrough.title: David's Punishment: Begging Cards
walkthrough.text: Let's see the consequences of David's premature family growth. He has three family members requiring six food total. David only managed to gather four food. He is two food short and must take two begging cards, costing him six victory points at game end.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Begging Cards Are Devastating
walkthrough.text: Let's appreciate how brutal begging cards are. Each one costs three victory points, and David just lost six points in a game where winning scores are often in the thirties. The lesson is clear: never grow your family until you can reliably produce enough food to feed everyone at every harvest.

walkthrough.title: Stage Two Begins
walkthrough.text: Let's enter stage two starting with round five. The round cards now reveal more powerful actions. Anna has a solid wood supply and starts building a third room to prepare for family growth. Ben has his Fireplace and begins taking sheep to cook into food.

walkthrough.title: Anna's Mistake: Ignoring Empty Spaces
walkthrough.text: Let's look at Anna's farmyard. She has been focused on collecting wood and building rooms but has not plowed any fields or built any pastures. Of her fifteen farmyard spaces, nine are completely empty. Each unused space will cost her one point at the end of the game.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Fill Your Farmyard
walkthrough.text: Let's understand the empty space penalty. Every unused farmyard space is negative one point at game end. With nine empty spaces, Anna would lose nine points. You need to fill your farm with a mix of fields, pastures, and rooms. Start plowing and fencing early so you are not desperately trying to fill spaces in the final rounds.

walkthrough.title: Mid-Game Check-In
walkthrough.text: Let's compare our four farmers. Clara is in the best shape with sown fields producing grain and pastures ready for animals. Ben has a food engine running through his Fireplace. Anna has workers but an empty farm. David is recovering from his begging cards but learning fast.

walkthrough.title: Key Takeaways
walkthrough.text: Let's review the three critical lessons from our practice game. First, build a food engine before growing your family. Second, always prepare pastures or stables before taking animals. Third, fill your farmyard with fields and pastures to avoid negative one penalties for empty spaces.

walkthrough.title: Before You Plow
walkthrough.text:
Before you start a full game, here are three things that can help.

First — check out General Tips for strategy basics and common mistakes to avoid.

Second — reference the Appendix for a quick guide to actions, resources, and scoring terms.

And most importantly — ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Now go build the best farm you can.

summary.title: Before You Plow
bullets: Check General Tips for strategy basics
Reference Appendix for actions, resources, scoring
Ask your personal GM Guide → tap Q&A for instant answers during play

walkthrough.title: Secure Food Early
walkthrough.text: Let's start with the most important tip in Agricola. Get a reliable food source set up in the first few rounds before anything else. A Fireplace or Cooking Hearth lets you convert animals to food anytime, and sown grain fields produce food every harvest. Without a food engine, you will spend the entire game scrambling instead of building.

walkthrough.title: Grow Your Family Strategically
walkthrough.text: Let's talk about when to add workers. A third family member by round five or six is ideal, but only if you can feed three people. A fourth worker is great if you can manage eight food per harvest. Each additional worker gives you one more action per round, which compounds over the remaining game.

walkthrough.title: Fill Every Farmyard Space
walkthrough.text: Let's remember the empty space penalty. Each unused space costs one point, and with fifteen total spaces, that can add up devastatingly fast. Plan your farm layout early: rooms on the left, fields in the middle, pastures on the right. Every space should be a room, field, or part of a pasture by game end.

walkthrough.title: Diversify Your Scoring
walkthrough.text: Let's look at scoring bands. Having zero of any category means negative points, while even a small amount gets you into positive territory. Aim to have at least one of each: some grain, some vegetables, some of each animal type, a few fields, and a pasture or two. Breadth beats depth in Agricola.

walkthrough.title: Watch Accumulation Timing
walkthrough.text: Let's master accumulation spaces. Resources pile up every round on spaces like wood and clay. Taking three wood on round one gets you three, but waiting until round three gets you nine. Balance the value of waiting against the risk that another player grabs the pile before you do.

walkthrough.title: Plan Around Harvest Rounds
walkthrough.text: Let's always keep the harvest schedule in mind. Harvests happen after rounds four, seven, nine, eleven, thirteen, and fourteen. Notice the gaps get shorter as the game progresses. Make sure you have enough food stockpiled or convertible before each harvest arrives.

walkthrough.title: Read Your Cards Early
walkthrough.text: Let's examine your hand from the start. Your seven occupations and seven minor improvements shape your entire strategy. Look for synergies between cards and plan your game around them. A card that gives bonus food changes your priorities completely compared to one that gives bonus points for animals.

walkthrough.title: Block When It Matters
walkthrough.text: Let's consider your opponents. If you see another player desperately needs a specific action space, taking it yourself can be a strong defensive play even if it is not your top priority. Blocking family growth or a crucial resource can slow down the leader. Just make sure the action still benefits you.

summary.title: Secure Food Early
bullets: Get Fireplace/Cooking Hearth or sow grain ASAP
No food engine → begging cards → game over

summary.title: Grow Your Family Strategically
bullets: 4th worker if you can sustain 8 food/harvest

summary.title: Fill Every Farmyard Space
bullets: Plan layout: rooms left, fields middle, pastures right

summary.title: Diversify Your Scoring
bullets: Breadth > depth — cover all scoring bands

summary.title: Watch Accumulation Timing
bullets: Bigger pile = better value but risk of opponent taking it

summary.title: Read Your Cards Early
bullets: Look for synergies between occupations + minor improvements

summary.title: Block When It Matters
bullets: Only block if the action also benefits you

walkthrough.title: The Reed Strategy
walkthrough.text: Let's explore an underrated resource. Reed is the scarcest resource in Agricola, needed for every room and renovation. Controlling the reed supply by taking it early and often can starve opponents of their ability to build rooms and grow their families. Pair this with an occupation that gives reed bonuses for maximum impact.

walkthrough.title: Oven Rush
walkthrough.text: Let's look at the oven strategy. Grab a Clay Oven or Stone Oven early, then plow and sow aggressively. Every time you take the Sow action, the oven gives you a large food bonus on top of the planted crops. This creates a self-sustaining food engine that lets you focus all other actions on building your farm.

walkthrough.title: Stone House Rush
walkthrough.text: Let's discuss the renovation path. Upgrading to stone rooms scores two points each instead of one for clay. If you can renovate twice, going wood to clay to stone, your rooms become a significant scoring engine. Combine this with family growth for three or four stone rooms worth six to eight bonus points.

walkthrough.title: Animal Breeding Engine
walkthrough.text: Let's master animal husbandry. Build large pastures with stables early, then take two animals of the same type. Every harvest, they breed automatically, giving you free animals. With fenced stables doubling capacity, a well-built pasture network can score maximum points in all three animal categories without spending many actions.

walkthrough.title: Five Worker Strategy
walkthrough.text: Let's push the worker count. With five family members, you take five actions per round while most opponents take three or four. This is incredibly powerful but requires fifteen food per harvest. You need a rock-solid food engine and an early third room. This strategy dominates in the mid-to-late game if you survive the food pressure.

walkthrough.title: Card Synergy Chains
walkthrough.text: Let's think about combos. The best Agricola players build chains of cards that amplify each other. An occupation that gives food when you plow plus a minor improvement that gives grain when you build fences creates compound value from single actions. Always look for cards in your hand that work together before committing to a strategy.

walkthrough.title: Start Player Tempo
walkthrough.text: Let's use the Start Player action wisely. Taking first player position is most valuable right before a critical round card appears or when you need to guarantee access to a contested action space. In the later stages when rounds are shorter and competition fiercer, being start player can make the difference between a clean harvest and begging cards.

walkthrough.title: End-Game Point Maximization
walkthrough.text: Let's optimize the final rounds. In rounds thirteen and fourteen, focus entirely on filling scoring gaps. Check each category: do you have at least some grain, vegetables, each animal type, fields, and pastures? Use your last actions to patch holes rather than improve strengths. Moving from zero to one in a category is often worth more than adding a sixth sheep.

summary.title: The Reed Strategy
bullets: Reed is scarcest resource — needed for rooms + renovation
Controlling reed starves opponents of family growth

summary.title: Oven Rush
bullets: Clay/Stone Oven → food bonus every time you sow
Plow + sow aggressively → self-sustaining food engine

summary.title: Stone House Rush
bullets: Stone rooms = +2 VP each vs +1 for clay

summary.title: Animal Breeding Engine
bullets: 2+ same animal → free baby each harvest
Compound growth covers all 3 animal categories

summary.title: Five Worker Strategy
bullets: Need rock-solid food engine + early rooms

summary.title: Card Synergy Chains
bullets: Combine occupations + minor improvements for compound value
Plan strategy around card combos before committing

summary.title: Start Player Tempo
bullets: Take start player before critical round cards
Most valuable in later stages when competition is fierce

summary.title: End-Game Point Maximization
bullets: 0 → 1 in a category often worth more than stacking strengths

entries.definition: Accumulation action space. Collect all wood tokens accumulated on this space. Replenishes with three wood each round.
Accumulation action space. Collect all clay tokens on this space. Replenishes with one clay each round.
Accumulation action space. Collect all reed tokens on this space. Replenishes with one reed each round.
Accumulation action space available in later stages. Collect all stone tokens. Replenishes with one stone each round.
Accumulation action space. Collect all food tokens accumulated here. Replenishes with one food each round.
Non-accumulating action space. Take two food immediately from the general supply.
Place one field tile on an empty farmyard space. Fields are required before you can sow grain or vegetables.
Place grain (three tokens) or vegetables (two tokens) from your supply onto plowed fields. Optionally bake bread if you have a baking improvement.
Pay wood to build fences around farmyard spaces, creating pastures. Each fence piece costs one wood. Multiple pastures may be built in one action.
Pay resources to add rooms to your house or stables to your farmyard. Rooms must be adjacent to existing rooms. Stables cost two wood each.
Add one new family member to an empty room. The new worker is available starting next round. Requires an unoccupied room.
Late-game round card action. Grow your family without needing an empty room. Only available in stage five or later.
Upgrade all rooms one level: wood to clay or clay to stone. Pay one of the new material per room plus one reed total. May also build a major or minor improvement.
Round card action. Take one sheep from the supply (or accumulation amount). Must have legal housing immediately.
Round card action appearing in later stages. Take one or more wild boar from the supply. Must have legal housing.
Round card action appearing in later stages. Take one cattle from the supply. Must have legal housing.
Take the start player token and receive one food. You will go first in all subsequent rounds until someone else takes this action.
Pay food to play one occupation card from your hand. Cost depends on player count: one food in a three or four player game.
Basic building resource. Used for wooden rooms (five per room), fences (one each), and stables (two each). Accumulates three per round.
Building and renovation resource. Used for clay rooms (five per room) and Fireplace improvements. Accumulates one per round.
Scarce construction resource. Required for all room builds (two per room) and renovations (one total). Accumulates one per round.
Premium building resource available in later stages. Used for stone rooms (five per room) and major improvements. Accumulates one per round.
Crop resource used for sowing fields and baking bread. When sown, three grain tokens are placed on a field. Can be converted to food via baking.
Crop resource used for sowing fields. When sown, two vegetable tokens are placed on a field. More valuable per token than grain for scoring.
Currency used to feed family members at harvest and to play occupation cards. Two food required per family member per harvest.
Livestock worth one food when cooked with a Fireplace or Cooking Hearth. Scores zero to eight points depending on quantity owned.
Livestock worth two food when cooked. Scores zero to seven points depending on quantity. Available from round card actions in later stages.
Livestock worth three food when cooked. Most valuable animal. Scores zero to six points depending on quantity. Available in later stages.
A plowed farmyard space where grain or vegetables can be sown. Scores zero to four points based on total number of fields.
A fully fenced area of one or more farmyard spaces. Holds two animals of one type per space. Scores zero to four points based on total number.
A structure costing two wood. Inside a pasture, doubles animal capacity. Unfenced, holds exactly one animal. Fenced stables score one bonus point each.
Starting room type. Costs five wood and two reed to build. Scores zero bonus points. Can be upgraded to clay via renovation.
Mid-tier room type. Costs five clay and two reed to build, or renovate from wood. Scores one bonus point per room.
Top-tier room type. Costs five stone and two reed to build, or renovate from clay. Scores two bonus points per room.
Your personal three-by-five grid of fifteen spaces. Each space can hold a room, field, pasture section, or stable. Unused spaces score negative one point each.
Player card providing ongoing abilities or bonuses. Costs food to play using the occupation action space. Each player starts with seven in hand.
Player card providing permanent farm upgrades. Costs various resources. Can be played from improvement action spaces or after certain actions. Seven dealt per player.
Shared improvement cards available to all players. Includes Fireplace, Cooking Hearth, Clay Oven, Stone Oven, Well, Joinery, and Pottery. Once claimed, unavailable to others.
Major improvement costing two clay. Allows converting animals and vegetables to food at any time. Sheep worth two food, boar worth two, cattle worth three.
Major improvement costing four clay or upgraded from Fireplace for one clay. Better food conversion rates than Fireplace. Core food engine card.
Major improvement costing three clay and one stone. When you take the Sow action, bake up to one grain into five food.
Major improvement costing one clay and three stone. When you take the Sow action, bake up to two grain into four food each. Slightly less per grain but handles more volume.
One of fourteen cards revealed one per round, opening new action spaces. Organized into six stages introducing sheep, fences, stone, boar, cattle, and family growth options.
End-of-stage event with three phases: field, feeding, and breeding. Occurs after rounds four, seven, nine, eleven, thirteen, and fourteen. Six harvests total.
First harvest phase. Remove one grain or vegetable token from each sown field and add it to your personal supply.
Second harvest phase. Pay two food per family member. Take one begging card for each food you cannot pay. The most stressful part of Agricola.
Third harvest phase. If you have two or more of the same animal type, gain one baby animal. Must have housing for the baby or it is lost.
Penalty card taken when you cannot fully feed your family. Each begging card is worth negative three victory points at game end.

Each scoring category uses a graduated scale. For example, zero to one grain scores negative one point, two to three scores one point, four to five scores two points, six to seven scores three points, eight or more scores four points.
Each empty farmyard space at game end costs one victory point. With fifteen total spaces and only two starting rooms, you must fill thirteen spaces to avoid any penalties.
Each family member scores three victory points at game end regardless of other factors. Growing from two to five family members nets nine extra points.
Each stable inside a fenced pasture scores one bonus victory point at game end, in addition to its functional benefit of doubling animal capacity.

One of six groups of rounds. Stage one has four rounds, stage two has three, stage three has two, stage four has two, stage five has two, and stage six has one. Harvest occurs at the end of each stage.
An action space that gains additional resources each round whether or not it is used. Resources stack until a player collects them all.
The main phase of each round where players take turns placing workers on action spaces. Proceeds clockwise from the start player.
The player who places the first worker each round. Can change when someone takes the Starting Player action space. Receives one food as a bonus.
One animal of any type may be kept in your house without pasture or stable. Only one pet total, not one per room.
Convert grain tokens into food using a baking improvement like Clay Oven or Stone Oven. Can be done as part of the Sow action or certain other actions.
Converting animals or vegetables into food using a cooking improvement like Fireplace or Cooking Hearth. Can be done at any time, not just during your turn.
